title: Sudden chest pain and breathlessness

chart.cc: Sudden chest pain and shortness of breath
chart.oneLiner: 22M, tall and thin, presents with the abrupt onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness while at rest.

opening: My right side started hurting really sharply out of nowhere and now I can't seem to catch my breath.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 22-year-old man, tall and thin (lanky build), mildly anxious and breathing a little fast, holding his right chest. Cooperative.

HPI: Abrupt onset about 2 hours ago, at rest while watching TV, of sharp RIGHT-sided chest pain that is worse with breathing (pleuritic) and a sensation of breathlessness. No trauma, no heavy lifting or exertion at onset. Pain does not radiate to the arm/jaw and is not pressure-like. NO fever, NO cough or purulent sputum, NO leg swelling or calf pain, NO recent long travel or immobilization, NO known heart or lung disease. Has had one similar but milder episode last year that resolved on its own. Smokes about half a pack a day (only if asked about tobacco). Recreational vaping/cannabis use occasionally (only if asked about substances). Family history: father had a 'collapsed lung' in his twenties (only if asked about family history).
PMH: none significant; tall/thin habitus. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: smoker (half pack/day), occasional vaping/cannabis, no alcohol problem. FAMILY: father with spontaneous 'collapsed lung' in youth (if asked).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tall, thin young man, mild respiratory distress, speaking in full sentences. Trachea midline (no deviation — this is NOT a tension pneumothorax).
- Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 104, RR 24, 37.0°C, SpO2 92% RA.
- Lungs: DECREASED/absent breath sounds over the RIGHT upper hemithorax, HYPERRESONANT to percussion on the right, DECREASED tactile fremitus on the right. Left lung clear with normal breath sounds.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur, no JVD; PMI not displaced. Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness. No chest wall tenderness to palpation, no crepitus.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the smoking, vaping/cannabis, the prior episode, or the family history unless asked about tobacco/substances, prior episodes, or family history respectively.
- Hyperresonance, decreased breath sounds, and decreased fremitus on the right are revealed ONLY if the corresponding exam is performed.
- Trachea is MIDLINE and the patient is hemodynamically stable; if asked, there is no tracheal deviation and no hypotension (rules against tension physiology).
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 8.4, Hgb 14.6, Plt 245 — within normal limits (no leukocytosis to suggest infection).
labs.Troponin: Undetectable (argues against acute coronary syndrome/myocardial injury).
labs.D-dimer: Normal/negative (low pretest probability; argues against pulmonary embolism).
labs.ABG: pH 7.46, PaCO2 33, PaO2 74 on room air — mild hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~104 bpm with no acute ischemic ST-T changes; normal axis and intervals.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia at ~104 bpm with no acute ischemic ST-T changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Right-sided pneumothorax: visible visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripheral to it in the right upper hemithorax, partial lung collapse, and NO mediastinal shift (no tension).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings, and no mediastinal shift. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing a right-sided pneumothorax with visible pleural line
searchTerms: visceral pleural line lung markings absent radiograph

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this acute chest pain and dyspnea (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Spontaneous pneumothorax (leading,8; accepts primary spontaneous pneumothorax). Tension pneumothorax (CANT-MISS to exclude,5). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS,4). Acute coronary syndrome / cardiac cause (core,2). Pneumonia/pleurisy (core,2). Musculoskeletal/costochondritis (bonus,2). Named leading dx with justification and the imperative to assess for tension physiology (tracheal deviation, hypotension) (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Spontaneous pneumothorax. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Tension pneumothorax; Pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: Acute coronary syndrome; Pneumonia/pleurisy; Musculoskeletal/costochondritis.

coreActions.item: Assesses for tension physiology (tracheal deviation, hypotension)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? (Consider what changes management and what excludes the must-not-miss diagnoses.)
steps.rubric: Upright chest X-ray (CANT-MISS,5). Continuous pulse oximetry / repeat vitals to monitor for deterioration (3). ABG if hypoxemic or not improving (2). EKG to evaluate for cardiac cause (2). D-dimer or risk-stratify for PE if features warrant (2). PENALTY: ordering a CT angiogram or extensive cardiac workup before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax (-2); delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging IF the patient becomes hemodynamically unstable/tension is suspected (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; Troponin; D dimer; ABG.

coreActions.item: ABG if hypoxemic or not improving
EKG to evaluate for cardiac cause

penalties.item: ordering CT/extensive cardiac workup before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax
delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging if tension physiology is suspected

optionalTests: ABG if hypoxemic or not improving
EKG to evaluate for cardiac cause
contraindicatedTests: ordering CT/extensive cardiac workup before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax
orderSensitiveActions: delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging if tension physiology is suspected

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes no acute ischemic ST changes (compensatory tachycardia from pain/hypoxia) (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia or right-heart-strain that is not present (-3).
keyFindings: states there are no acute ischemic ST-T changes
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes.
commonMistakes: over-calling ischemia from rate-related repolarization changes
inventing a right-heart-strain pattern that is not present
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~104 bpm with no acute ischemic ST-T changes; normal axis and intervals.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes.
coreActions: states there are no acute ischemic ST-T changes

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = right pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line and absent peripheral lung markings; NO mediastinal shift. CREDIT: identifies the pneumothorax / visceral pleural line (6); explicitly comments on the absence of mediastinal shift (rules out tension) (4). PENALTY: missing the pneumothorax / calling the film normal (-3).
keyFindings: identifies a visible visceral pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings
notes there is no mediastinal/tracheal shift (no tension)
criticalFindings: Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings).
commonMistakes: missing a subtle apical pneumothorax / calling the film normal
mistaking a skin fold or bulla for the pleural line
steps.idealAnswer: Right-sided pneumothorax: visible visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripherally and partial lung collapse; no mediastinal shift, so no tension physiology.

criticalActions: Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings).
coreActions: identifies a visible visceral pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings
notes there is no mediastinal/tracheal shift (no tension)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The chest X-ray shows a right-sided pneumothorax with a visceral pleural line and no mediastinal shift; the patient remains hemodynamically stable with a midline trachea, troponin is negative, and D-dimer is normal. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (6). Explicitly excludes tension pneumothorax (stable vitals, midline trachea, no mediastinal shift) and pulmonary embolism / ACS (negative D-dimer and troponin) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Tension pneumothorax is excluded by the stable vitals, midline trachea, and absence of mediastinal shift; pulmonary embolism and ACS are argued against by the normal D-dimer and negative troponin.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH pneumothorax management: supplemental high-flow oxygen to speed pleural reabsorption (CANT-MISS,5); size/symptom-based intervention — observation for a small asymptomatic primary pneumothorax vs needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube/pigtail catheter for a large or symptomatic one (CANT-MISS,6); analgesia (3); admit/observe with serial imaging (3); smoking cessation counseling (3); thoracic surgery consult and discussion of pleurodesis for recurrence/persistent air leak (3); immediate needle decompression THEN chest tube if tension develops (bonus,2). PENALTY: discharging a large symptomatic pneumothorax without intervention or follow-up (-3); failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax: give high-flow supplemental oxygen (hastens pleural air reabsorption); choose intervention by size/symptoms — observe a small, asymptomatic pneumothorax with repeat imaging, or perform needle aspiration or place a small-bore chest tube/pigtail catheter for a large or symptomatic one. Provide analgesia, admit/observe with serial CXR, and counsel on smoking cessation. Consult thoracic surgery and discuss pleurodesis/VATS for recurrence or a persistent air leak. If tension physiology develops, perform immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube.

coreActions.item: Thoracic surgery consult / pleurodesis discussion for recurrence

bonusActions.item: Immediate needle decompression then chest tube if tension develops

penalties.item: Discharging a large symptomatic pneumothorax without intervention or follow-up
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging

optionalActions: Thoracic surgery consult / pleurodesis discussion for recurrence
Immediate needle decompression then chest tube if tension develops
unsafeActions: Discharging a large symptomatic pneumothorax without intervention or follow-up
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging
steps.disposition: Admit; immediate decompression and ICU if tension physiology develops

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues
standardizedPatient.affect: 22-year-old man, tall and thin, mildly anxious and breathing a little fast, holding his right chest. Cooperative.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 22M, tall and thin, presents with the abrupt onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness at rest. The diagnosis is Spontaneous pneumothorax, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Lungs: DECREASED/absent breath sounds over the RIGHT upper hemithorax. Left lung clear with normal breath sounds.
Lungs: HYPERRESONANT to percussion over the right hemithorax.
Lungs: DECREASED tactile fremitus over the right hemithorax.
General: tall, thin young man in mild respiratory distress, speaking in full sentences. Trachea midline (no deviation — NOT a tension pneumothorax).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Spontaneous pneumothorax while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Spontaneous pneumothorax and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Discharging a large symptomatic pneumothorax without intervention or follow-up
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes.
Right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line and absent peripheral lung markings; no mediastinal shift (no tension).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes.
Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes.
Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
